meta.note: 80-150 word answers in Jamie's voice. Honest about being a career-pivot into direct student support. Adapted from Vestas DEI, Global Shapers, Education Studies, and InGenius point-of-contact work.

answers.question: Why are you interested in this role?
answers.answer: I'm a Portland resident, and the idea of doing this work for students in my own community genuinely excites me. My background is in people-centered program work—I treat support as something you design around the person, not the other way around. What draws me to the Basic Needs Center specifically is that it sits right where my values and my skills meet: lowering barriers to food, housing, and benefits for the students who need it most, often the ones who find it hardest to ask. I'll be candid that I'm pivoting into direct student support from adjacent roles, but the core of the work—meeting people with dignity, navigating systems on their behalf, and coordinating resources—is exactly what I've been doing and want to keep doing.

answers.question: Describe your experience supporting students or underserved communities.
answers.answer: I'll be honest that I haven't been a direct-service advisor in higher ed, so let me share where my experience genuinely translates. Through Global Shapers, an NGO under the World Economic Forum, I do volunteer community work focused on lowering barriers for newcomers—building onboarding support, a buddy system, and feedback loops so people feel supported rather than left to figure things out alone. In my current role at InGenius Prep, I'm the primary point of contact for staff and students, guiding them through program resources, eligibility, and intake one person at a time. My BA in Education Studies and my consulting work have kept me close to how people learn, grow, and navigate institutions—and how often the system itself is the barrier.

answers.question: How do you approach equity and inclusion in your work?
answers.answer: I treat equity as a systems question, not a sentiment. My graduate training in Organizational Psychology taught me to ask who an existing process actually works for, and who it quietly leaves out. At Vestas, I initiated a DEI pilot with an Inclusive Leadership workshop that grew from 12 senior leaders to 23 global locations—I built awareness first, found an internal champion, then made the change practical and durable. At NextGen Healthcare, I delivered training on DEI and Inclusive Leadership and analyzed 2,000+ employee survey responses specifically to surface voices that usually go unheard. My approach is always the same: name the cultural and structural barriers honestly, listen to the people closest to them, and redesign so access isn't an accident.
